author: @SchwaAlien
text: About 20 years ago I watched a rail freight terminal get replaced by a commercial business park and I wanted to lease a unit to start an electronics recycling company that recovered resources, I figured there was probably enough value in various common materials found like silver and gold, as well as trying to salvage / refurbish working equipment since a lot of what gets recycled is just “obsolete” rather than malfunctioning, but I had no funds for that, and years later once I did, of course I had no time because my job was more of a contractor arrangement where I would work on installations to get them completed rather than just for a certain amount of time, so some days I’d come home very late and other days I would be finished early, completely unpredictable though...  It would have been fun to learn about all the elements of the various equipment, like the optical glass and various coatings in the LCD panels, as a TV installer I had access to stacks and stacks of large panels that had malfunctioned but were in good physical shape...  I had collected a bunch in a storage locker at one point and then someone broke in an stole all the good working ones so I had to cut my losses and took most of them that remained to recycling because I’m in small place that already has 3 TVs, but I’m moving to a smaller community soon where it may be possible to operate a side business with the job I’ll have driving a refrigerated truck once a week; I did save a stack of the good working heavy hifi amplifiers the thieves didn’t take, people always appreciate old high-end audio equipment because they built those things to last, I’ll start with them and a few working panels I packed away, but it’ll be interesting to see what people recycle there.

author: @RWBHere
text:
I was one of the designers and makers of a bipolar RF power transistor which was shipped in a similar TO-3 package to the 2N3055, except that the tab was thicker, and Nickel-plated Copper, rather than the Aluminium of the Motorola device, and the leads were slightly larger in diameter, because of the much higher currents involved. The chip was close to the maximum size which would fit into that type of package, and about 50% of the Silicon was not covered by connector pads. At least a milliamp could be generated very easily from factory lighting, which meant that testing and selection of the raw chips had to be done under low light conditions. Also, if a chip was being tested in an uncapped header, whilst exposed to light, the photo-current generated around the Base could be amplified to hundreds of milliamps through the Collector. 

The photovoltaic effect is actively used in some transistors, to this day. You can buy phototransistors, as well as opto-isolators, and various types of sensors which incorporate an LED and a phototransistor. There are also photodiodes which detect light without amplifying the current generated. You can also find photodiodes and phototransistors in the transmitters and receivers of TV remote control systems.

Epoxy-covered transistors of the flat type you showed can have various pinouts, such as BCE, CEB, CBE, ECB, etc, depending upon the design constraints, application and original customer's requirements. There are also various sizes of package, as you might expect.
Many SM PSU's also have full wave rectifier pairs, and even full wave bridge rectifiers, in similar packages, as well as MOSFETs, in similar standard packages, so be sure to check what you are really dealing with when you repurpose them.

author: @hanslepoeter5167
text: That's a lot of fun. Back in the early days of transistors you would have glass transistors usually painted black. You could just scrape the paint off and use them as photo transistors. OC71 was a good example. For anyone thinking of creating their own solar panel using scrap transistors I highly recommend against it. The generated power will be next to nothing and the effort would be huge.

author: @wrekced
text:
Very interesting! I am going to try that on some transistors I have.
Here are some ideas I have about decapping (look that word up on youtube and watch the videos about decapping IC's to access their RAM) the power transistors: The plastic that is used for the body of some of those power transistors can be disolved with MEK (methyl ethyl ketone or butanone). Just a couple drops in the right place should expose the Silicon. MEK is available from some hardware stores in the US. MEK is a very strong solvent and can be hazardous to use. It is something that you definitely want to read about before using. I'd start with the MSDS (material safety data sheet) and then any other safety and handling info you can find on the internet. However, with MEK, it is easy to disolve the epoxy cases. The ceramic cases are probably openable with good (diamond, zircon or ceramic grit) sandpaper on a belt or disk sander. Heat would be the problem there. If you hold the transistor with a finger on it and dunk it in some cool water when it gets warm; I think the silicon would survive. You would have to do the first few a little layer at a time to find out how much case you need to remove to expose the silicon.
I wonder how much power a large metal-cased transistor would produce under a magnifying glass in the sun? If you use a low power lens and connect the transistor to a huge heatsink, it might be possible to use one that way to power something.

author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
text:
I actually have an idea for gate transistors that can be swapped (ie turned from an NPN to a PNP version, as well as changing the variable state or quantity of holes and electrons thus increasing the density of these or decreasing them in 3 increments basically creating a transistor that can be altered on the fly (ie while its in a CPU say this can be changed constantly for both storage and computational arrangements) to allow jumping from on and off states for a number of options being only binary or 2 (0 and 1) to being able to be altered to a dual polarity quaternary gate or options being -3, -2, -1,0,1,2, and 3 though in this system 0 is not off but fully on with no bias, which I think the system would then be stacking micro voltage pressure resistances to being turned on and off when in a "switch" state, and counting up or stacking the resistance caused by the fully negated components (ie turning it into a tiny resistor, which I would guess that a small alteration would allow making it able to be also a memsistor with variable resistances but no bias)  Just thought I would drop that here and see if anyone wants to have a discussion. 

I had a long bit typed out about how and what brought me to that reasoning, but I will just say, it was some ideas I had, then later had confirmation it CAN be done because it seems someone has done it already, though for other reasons related to the oil industry and this has inspired me to continue developing other ideas off of that same tech and core fabrication techniques.
    Anyway as I said I had a long bit typed out, but I will save it for a video and try and come back to drop a link and see if you want to do a series of response videos or something, discussing various methods and ideas for electronic component fabrication, as well as will post videos of the stuff I am actually trying.

author: @will_doherty
text:
The idea and execution of the theory are as usual excellent, however again you've stumbled over the open circuit voltage being meaningful, when it's not much of an indicator of anything.

I suspect that your calculation of powe output is a magnitude or two awry, had you measured the voltage alongside the current, you'd have had a valid result

As you say, compared to a solar panel size for size, it may have potential - but if it were viable (and superior) it's inconceivable that the manufacturers wouldn't already be reproducing it at scale...

author: @DeliciousDeBlair
text:
If you have access to a really strong pulsed laser diode, you might could cut your little transistors apart without damaging them.
If they can not be used any other way and if they have no other use, you have no risk if you break them, but it might work.
Other than that, secure them firmly to a stable holder [preferably something that is set up to be rapidly reusable] and grind off the part you do not want with a grinder wheel of some kind until you reveal the part you want to keep.
If any of you are familiar with a surface grinder like the one in this image [used only for the purpose of illustration of a CONCEPT, not suggesting one waste time and money on such an expensive tool just for this project!] ( https://image.made-in-china.com/2f0j00JEiUKFAaAckR/Simple-Surface-Grinder-Small-Universal-Tool-Grinder-Manual-Surface-Grinding-Machine-Mj7115.jpg  ) the idea is that a relatively stable [yet movable] flat table-like surface is made [with the advent of 3-D printing, movable tables like this, of a suitable quality can be had relatively cheaply] where a grinder wheel is set at a fixed height, and said table is located beneath it.
If one can get hold of a decent small bench/pedestal grinder [again, image for illustration purposes only ( https://images.homedepot-static.com/productImages/a75df812-0b22-44c5-ba9f-2764735f1fb0/svn/dewalt-bench-grinders-dw758-64_1000.jpg  )] from some source such as e-bay, craigslist, amazon, or perhaps your local flea market, and set it up on a large enough bench or tale, along with their small sliding table [ again, image for reference only, please note that in this image, only the Y-axis relates to the table, vs both X & Y axes, but the point should be sufficient ( https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2214860418303968-gr1.jpg ) ] which is positioned carefully to an appropriate elevation beneath an exposed grinder wheel, should allow one to very carefully and reliably grind off one layer of a transistor, leaving one with only the desired part for their experimentation, and be able to do so reliably, especially if they always use transistors of the same relative thicknesses, and perhaps use some kind of leveling screws to adjust the height as well as to ensure that the cut is flat and true.

author: @RicardoPenders
text:
what if you'd take a bunch of Peltier elements and use those on the backside of solar panels to get rid of the heat and at the same time generate extra power, to make it more efficient you stick a big ass heatsink on the cool side of the Peltier elements, hot side to the back of the solar panel?
Peltier elements are not very efficient but in this application it doesn't really matter as much because you use them as heatsink and the power generated is extra.

author: @tebbi67
text:
You are my laboratory hero, this attempt I had carried out exactly the same 20 years ago and had ignored the consideration to make the heat over the diode sections, to current with another semiconductor layer. What luck it gives you! 
You can get the old horizontal deflection transistors from the CRT technology in large batches quite cheaply.
You gave me a good idea. 
Thanks for the video.

replies.text: Like you Mark, I too collected any dumped microwaves, I've had about 10, many were just dumped in the scenic countryside but all I wanted were the 2 magnets and the frilly brass circular thing on the magnatron. Any idea what that is please? I'm also very careful not to scratch the pink or light blue area's because that could cause you some major lung issues if breathed in and wrap it in tape before going to scrap.

author: @Killianwsh
text: Hey Rob, great video! I did not know how easily one could access light sensitive bits in side those transistors!  I'd imagine that you could affix a tubular shroud a few inches long (& coated black inside) to the case to shield it from "triggering" until sunlight shone directly down the tube. Then you could use it to align solar concentration and photovoltaic tracking systems to a very high degree of accuracy and further increase the output.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: that would work for sure - nice one mate

replies.author: @SinsBird
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering You could do 2 quick simple tests if curious:
1. Send full current that the datasheet specifies.
2 Send full voltage.
Don't give it full voltage and full current at the same time. Even a real transistor might pop.
If transistor pops during one of these tests, it's fake.

replies.text: @valveman12 What, why?

author: @gerritmarskamp8030
text: Hello, i used to perform these experiments when i was much younger, decapitating bc 108, 2n3055 and the like. But; i was aware that some of the older types, may contain Beryllium Oxide  (Beo) which is highly toxic !! So do some investigating before opening these transistors at random.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I am sorry mate - and I don't mean to be rude - but I never listen to may contain - that's an ass covering statement if ever I heard one lol - like may contain peanuts. - these semiconductors may contain peanuts for all I know - so all you nut allergy folks out there - watch out lol

replies.author: @gerritmarskamp8030
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Hello Robert,  o i don´t feel offended, but maybe i should not have used  the term "may contain" (as English is not my native language ) To put it more in context, in the data sheet for the Bly 89 from philips, page 2 (link below in my message) It mentions that indeed this one incorporates it and that is toxic. It is specially toxic when inhaled after crushing. Now is this type not that likely to be found or opened, but i am sure there are some others still around. This one was around in 1986 Link: pdf.datasheetcatalog.com/datasheet/philips/BLY89.pdf Greetings and thank you for responding

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I am sorry mate - I just saw the funny side and was trying to share a joke with you - I didn't mean to be rude

replies.author: @gerritmarskamp8030
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Me being a hardware nerd (for the greater part) i missed that. But i will inform my colleague tomorrow, not to open any transistors, as she is highly allergic to peanuts :-)
